bibliographic.citation: Edith Rickert, ed., The Romance of Emaré: Re-edited from the MS with Introduction, Notes, and Glossary (Chicago: Richard Clay & Sons, Limited, 1907).
bibliographic.title: The Romance of Emaré

entities.description: The immediate print source for CME00009: Rickert's revised dissertation edition of the unique manuscript text, with introduction, marginal prose summaries, textual footnotes, endnotes, glossary, and index.
entities.label: Rickert, The Romance of Emaré (Chicago dissertation, 1907)
notes: Rickert says the edition was prepared in 1898-1899, then completely revised before publication; this is edition history, not a date of the medieval work.

The text occupies printed pp. 1-32. CME00009 retains its marginal summaries and page-level footnotes but omits the preface, fifty-page introduction, endnotes on pp. 33-48, glossary, index, and colophon.
The separately cataloged EETS issue carries confusing 1906/1908 publication history and is not silently equated here with the exact 1907 Chicago title page encoded by CME00009.

rights.basis: The University of Michigan item presenting this edition-derived material expressly states that the materials are in the public domain and applies No Copyright-United States.
rights.component: Rickert's 1907 edition, introduction, notes, and apparatus
rights.jurisdiction: United States
notes: No Copyright-United States is jurisdiction-specific and is not a worldwide license or dedication.

access.accessMethod: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue plus reader registration and item request; consult current restrictions with Reference Services
access.entityLabel: British Library, Cotton MS Caligula A II

access.accessMethod: Date-bounded review of the current British Library parent and component records, BL digitised-manuscript discovery, DIME, Biblissima/Arlima discovery, and the Siege of Jerusalem Electronic Archive
access.entityLabel: British Library, Cotton MS Caligula A II
access.format: No complete public digital object located
notes: The British Library records expose no digitised-content link.
DIME says only the Siege of Jerusalem folios have been digitised; those public images cover ff. 111r-125r and omit Emaré on ff. 71r-76v.

No public object covering the complete physical codex, covers, endleaves, blanks, adjacent works, and both codicological units was found. This is a dated negative search result, not proof that no private or institutional surrogate exists.

access.accessMethod: British Library Digitisation Services online ordering, subject to preservation review, feasibility, quote, payment, and use terms
access.entityLabel: British Library, Cotton MS Caligula A II
access.format: Digital reference or reproduction photography as approved
notes: A request for only 'Emaré' could produce a work excerpt rather than a complete-source facsimile. Request the entire physical codex and all offered exterior and endleaving surfaces, then identify ff. 71r-76v as the work portion.
The service states that original manuscripts are photographed rather than scanned and that reproduction permission is a separate question.

dateStatements.label: first manuscript unit
dateStatements.notes: DIME adopts the narrow dating also argued by Rickert from Lydgate's Nightingale and the chronicle additions.

dateStatements.label: current British Library catalog date
dateStatements.value: Second half of the fifteenth century

entities.description: A bound paper volume of two historically independent manuscript units. The first unit, ff. 3-139, is a Middle English miscellany containing the unique witness of Emaré on ff. 71r-76v; the second, ff. 142-210, contains Carthusian statutes and related records.

holding.notes: The exact component record for the Middle English miscellany is https://searcharchives.bl.uk/catalog/041-001102312.

entities.label: British Library, Cotton MS Caligula A II
notes: The British Library parent and component records expose no current digitised-content link.
DIME reports only the Siege of Jerusalem folios as digitised; the public Siege archive offers images of ff. 111r-125r, not Emaré.

A complete reproduction request should ask for front and back covers or boards, spine if offered, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, inserted or detached material, and every recto and verso through f. 210, then map Emaré separately to ff. 71r-76v.

physicalDescription.dimensions: Bound volume approximately 225 x 180 mm; DIME reports the first unit's leaves as approximately 210 x 140 mm
physicalDescription.extent: 210 folios: early-modern endleaves ff. 1-2; first manuscript unit ff. 3-139; early-modern leaves ff. 140-141; second manuscript unit ff. 142-210
notes: British Museum binding dated 1957.

The complete physical object contains covers, endleaves, blanks or inserted leaves, and many works adjacent to Emaré; these are not part of the intellectual work but belong in any complete-codex facsimile.
Emaré occupies ff. 71r-76v in the current foliation. Rickert and Gough record the legacy range as ff. 69-74.
physicalDescription.support: Paper; early-modern endleaves and inserted leaves; a parchment wrapper is reported for the second historical unit

rights.basis: The text is a medieval anonymous work attested in a fifteenth-century manuscript; this assessment does not determine rights in editions, encodings, transcriptions, catalog text, or photographs.
rights.component: The medieval wording of Emaré
rights.jurisdiction: Worldwide copyright-duration assessment

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a copy/version of University of Michigan CME item CME00009. That digital text manually represents the title page and the 1,035-line poem in Edith Rickert's 1907 Chicago dissertation, while omitting her preface, introduction, endnotes, glossary, index, colophon, and other book matter. Rickert transcribed the unique witness in British Library Cotton MS Caligula A II, ff. 71r-76v, with modern capitals and punctuation, marked expansions, additions, corrections, and apparatus consulting Ritson and Gough. Complete downloadable scans of Rickert, Gough, and Ritson's relevant volumes, plus White's later diplomatic transcription, are cached locally. No complete public digital object was found for the 210-folio physical codex: the only public manuscript photographs located cover the unrelated Siege of Jerusalem on ff. 111r-125r, omit Emaré, and are view-only under terms that prohibit downloading.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Late fourteenth century; uniquely attested in a manuscript copied 1446-1460
catalogSummary.region: Composition place uncertain: Northeast Midlands or East Anglia; the unique copy is assigned to the South-East or South-East Midlands
catalogSummary.title: Emaré

catalogingSubject.label: Emaré
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous late-fourteenth-century Middle English tail-rhyme romance, not Cotton MS Caligula A II as a physical miscellany, Rickert's 1907 scholarly edition, its prior or later editions, the U-M digital encoding, the repository XML artifact, or modern translations and adaptations.

assertion.notes: DIME gives the late fourteenth century; the range is not normalized to exact years because the cited sources do not supply them.

notes: Do not replace this work-level estimate with Rickert's 1907 publication date or the unique witness's fifteenth-century copying date.

assertion.notes: The current British Library component record gives the broader second half of the fifteenth century; DIME and Rickert support the narrower 1446-1460 range.

extent.description: The XML poem contains 86 numbered LG stanza groups and 1,035 direct L elements, matching DIME's 1,035-line description of the unique witness. It also contains 32 printed-page milestones, 345 line-number milestones, 12 manuscript-leaf milestones, 105 editorial footnotes, and 283 marginal prose summaries. Those notes and milestones describe Rickert's edition and the encoding, not additional intrinsic poem parts.

formStatements.description: Narrative verse in twelve-line tail-rhyme stanzas, normally described by DIME as aa4b3cc4b3dd4b3ee4b3, with metrical and rhyme-scheme variations.

assertion.notes: The closing stanza calls the story one of the lays of Britain; modern scholarship catalogs it among the Middle English Breton lays.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The XML's coarse TEXT/@LANG='eng' is not treated as evidence that the medieval poem is modern English.
Current foliation places Emaré on ff. 71r-76v; Rickert and Gough record legacy foliation ff. 69-74. These are two locator systems for one physical witness.
The work's language, composition date, and possible dialect region are not inherited from Rickert's Chicago imprint or the unique witness's modern holding location.
The complete-source facsimile question and the separate rights for manuscript photographs, editions, encoding, and repository artifact are recorded in the linked lineage manifest.

next_steps: Review current linguistic work that distinguishes authorial dialect from the South-East or South-East Midlands language of the unique fifteenth-century copy.
Preserve competing localizations as attributed claims rather than converting dialect evidence into an exact place of composition.
openQuestions.question: Can Emaré's composition date and original dialect or place be established more narrowly than late fourteenth century and Northeast Midlands or East Anglia?

next_steps: Review post-Rickert comparative work on the Constance or accused-queen traditions and the Breton-lay claim in lines 1030-1032.
Do not create a specific lost French-source entity from Rickert's linguistic and analogical reconstruction unless later transmission evidence supports it.
openQuestions.question: What source or source tradition immediately underlies the Middle English Emaré?

assertion.notes: DIME says the place of composition is uncertain; Laskaya and Salisbury describe late-fourteenth-century Northeast Midlands or East Anglian dialect features.

notes: The unique witness's South-East or South-East Midlands origin is a different, physical-copy layer recorded in lineage.
placeStatements.placeLabel: Northeast Midlands or East Anglia, England

assertion.notes: No author name is supplied by the unique witness or accepted by the scholarly sources used here.

subjects.label: Exile, sea voyages, separation, and family reunion

summaries.value: Emaré, daughter of Emperor Artyus, refuses her father's attempt to marry her and is set adrift wearing a marvelous gemmed and embroidered robe. Rescued in Galys, she marries its king, but his mother intercepts and forges letters after Emaré bears Segramour, causing mother and child to be cast out at sea again. They reach Rome, where Emaré supports them through needlework. Her husband and later her repentant father come to Rome on pilgrimage or penance; Segramour helps reveal her identity, and the separated family is reconciled.

The poem survives uniquely in Cotton MS Caligula A II, ff. 71r-76v, among many religious, didactic, medical, historical, and romance texts. CME00009 does not represent the complete manuscript or complete Rickert dissertation: it encodes the dissertation title page and the poem on printed pp. 1-32, including Rickert's marginal summaries and page footnotes.

assertion.notes: The manuscript explicit spells the name Emare; the acute accent is established modern title practice.

notes: Expansion letters follow Rickert's edited reading; punctuation is editorial.
titles.value: Ihesu, þat ys kyng in trone, As þou shoope boþe sonne and mone

work.author: Anonymous
work.dateDisplay: Late fourteenth century; uniquely attested in a manuscript copied 1446-1460

publication.embeddedTitle: The University of Chicago FOUNDED BY JOHN D. ROCKEFELLER

work.regionDisplay: Composition place uncertain: Northeast Midlands or East Anglia; the unique copy is assigned to the South-East or South-East Midlands

regions.label: Northeast Midlands or East Anglia, England
